items.headline: Cancer Drug Reduces Elevated Brain Connectivity in Early Alzheimer’s, Lab Study Finds
items.preview: A study reveals that eFT508, a cancer drug, may mitigate early synaptic hyperconnectivity in Alzheimer’s.
items.article:
In a groundbreaking study published in Translational Psychiatry, neuroscientists at King’s College London have illuminated a critical early mechanism driving neural hyperconnectivity in the nascent stages of Alzheimer’s disease. This revelation challenges long-standing theories about Alzheimer’s pathogenesis, introducing the possibility that the disease’s onset may be marked not by synapse loss, but by an exuberant and disorganized synaptic proliferation.
Why this matters: The findings suggest novel therapeutic targets that might forestall cognitive decline associated with mild cognitive impairment (MCI). The study identified alterations in 49 specific proteins following amyloid-beta exposure, indicating a coordinated molecular cascade initiating hyperconnectivity. Notably, the drug eFT508 significantly suppressed synaptic overgrowth and reversed many proteomic changes, positioning it as a promising candidate for repurposing in early Alzheimer’s treatment.
Leading this investigation, Professor Karl Peter Giese emphasized the innovative therapeutic implications, proposing early intervention targeting synaptic protein production can normalize hyperconnectivity and possibly delay cognitive impairment.

items.headline: AI Enables Early Prediction of Serious Heart Disease Using Mammogram Data
items.preview: AI technology can now predict cardiovascular risk using routine mammogram data, enhancing early detection.
items.article:
A groundbreaking study published in the European Heart Journal reveals an innovative application of artificial intelligence (AI) that could revolutionize cardiovascular disease detection, particularly in women. Researchers have developed an AI-based system capable of quantifying breast arterial calcifications, a critical marker of cardiovascular risk, directly from standard mammographic images.
Why this matters: This approach allows for the identification of women at risk of heart attacks and strokes without additional costs or procedures. The AI model demonstrated a strong correlation between the extent of calcification and cardiovascular risk, even in younger women under 50, challenging existing paradigms about risk stratification. Integrating this technology into routine mammography could significantly enhance preventive cardiology efforts, ensuring timely identification and management of cardiovascular disease in a traditionally underserved population.
